summary: The BeltLine's new I-20 bridge and Segment 6 overhaul are closer than you think, affordable housing along the corridor hits 79% of goal, and a federal judge saves $14.5 million in Atlanta homeless funding. Plus: Georgia's legislature wraps a messy Sine Die, Atlanta throws a 404 Day party, and PUMA brings World Cup energy Downtown.

topStory.headline: BeltLine's New I-20 Bridge and Southside Trail Overhaul Coming This Year
topStory.summary: The Southside Trail's ribbon-cutting is set for April 16, while Segment 6 between Memorial Drive and Glenwood Avenue gets a major upgrade starting this summer.
topStory.body:
Big changes are coming to one of the most popular stretches of BeltLine trail on Atlanta's eastside, and sooner than most users realize.

Atlanta BeltLine Inc. construction leaders announced during an update meeting Thursday that the Southside Trail segments between Glenwood Avenue and Boulevard are on pace for an official ribbon-cutting on April 16. The rest of the loop connecting around to the Westside should be open before mid-June.

But the bigger news is what comes next. Much of the Southside Trail's Segment 6, a half-mile section along Bill Kennedy Way stretching from Memorial Drive down to Glenwood Avenue, is set for a phased overhaul that could begin this summer, following Atlanta's 2026 FIFA World Cup matches.

That section has been operating in an interim status since 2020. The current setup includes an asphalt trail less than eight feet wide in places (far narrower than the BeltLine's standard 14 feet) and temporary Interstate 20 bridge lanes protected by moveable Jersey barriers.

The overhaul will replace those temporary crossings with a permanent bridge structure and widen the trail to full BeltLine standards. For the thousands of runners, cyclists, and commuters who use this corridor daily between Reynoldstown and Ormewood Park, the upgrade is long overdue.

The April 16 ribbon-cutting for the southern segments marks another milestone in completing the full 22-mile BeltLine loop, which has been under construction in phases for over a decade.
topStory.imageAlt: Rendering of the new BeltLine Southside Trail bridge over Interstate 20

stories.headline: BeltLine Reaches 79% of Its Affordable Housing Goal
stories.summary: Atlanta BeltLine Inc. says 4,425 affordable units have been delivered within the BeltLine corridor, with some residents paying less than $100 a month in rent.
stories.body:
The Atlanta BeltLine's affordable housing push is ahead of schedule, with 4,425 units delivered within the BeltLine Tax Allocation District. That puts the initiative at 79% of its goal to create or preserve 5,600 affordable housing units by 2030.

The BeltLine and its partners delivered 299 affordable units in 2025 and are preparing to break ground on more in 2026. Since 2018, the corridor has more than doubled its number of completed affordable units, with nearly three-quarters reserved for people earning 60% or less of the Area Median Income.

One example is The Madison Reynoldstown, an apartment complex along Memorial Drive in southeast Atlanta. All 116 units in the building are designated for residents with low to moderate incomes. Some residents pay less than $100 a month in rent.

"We're going to keep going, understanding the demand far exceeds 5,600 units," said Dennis Richards, Vice President of Housing for the Atlanta BeltLine.

Major projects that advanced in 2025 include 356 University Avenue, 350 Chappell Road, 425 Chappell Road, Murphy Crossing, and 579 Garson Drive. In January 2026, ABI launched a new Affordable Housing Dashboard and web-based mobile app to improve transparency around progress.

The numbers are encouraging, though the gap between supply and demand along one of the country's hottest real estate corridors remains enormous.
stories.imageAlt: 356 University Avenue, one of the affordable housing projects completed by Atlanta BeltLine Inc.

stories.headline: Pie Bar Planning New Cafe at Westside Market
stories.summary: The Woodstock-born pie shop is targeting a May opening for its first Atlanta location, with pies by the slice and espresso drinks.
stories.body:
Pie Bar is bringing a new cafe to Westside Market this year, marking the brand's first step into the Atlanta market proper.

Owner Dominie Garcia, who took over Pie Bar in October, told What Now Atlanta that the shop is targeting a May opening. The brand currently has three locations in Woodstock, Marietta Square, and Kennesaw.

The new location will serve pies alongside espresso beverages. Garcia said the in-store selection will be smaller than the existing shops, but customers can order any pie option ahead for pickup. The menu includes sweet options like lemon meringue, apple streusel, and peanut butter cream, plus take-and-bake savory pies including chicken pot pie and shepherd's pie.

The expansion to Westside Market represents what Garcia described as the start of organic growth for the brand as she looks to expand Pie Bar's reach across Atlanta.
stories.imageAlt: Pie Bar storefront, expanding to Westside Market in West Midtown

stories.headline: Federal Judge Blocks HUD Homeless Grants Overhaul, Protecting $14.5M for Atlanta
stories.summary: A federal ruling preserves Atlanta's Continuum of Care funding that keeps 844 formerly homeless households housed through permanent supportive housing programs.
stories.body:
A federal judge ruled this week that the Trump administration's attempt to rewrite the rules governing nearly $3.9 billion in Continuum of Care grants for permanent supportive housing was unlawful. The decision is a major relief for Atlanta's homeless services network.

At stake for Atlanta was $14.5 million in federal funding that keeps 844 formerly homeless households housed. Partner for Home, the city's homeless services liaison, uses the HUD grants to fund permanent housing and supportive services citywide.

HUD had issued an edict last September that it would not renew grant funding for programs following the long-accepted Housing First approach, which provides unhoused people with stable housing so they can address sobriety, mental health, and self-sufficiency. Instead, HUD wanted to redirect the funds toward programs requiring people to get addiction or mental health treatment before receiving housing.

U.S. District Judge Mary McElroy of Rhode Island ruled March 31 that HUD violated the Administrative Procedure Act by failing to provide a reasoned basis for its overhaul of funding criteria set by Congress. She called the sudden policy shift a "slapdash" effort.

The ruling means Atlanta's existing Housing First programs can continue operating with federal support, at least for now. The city's permanent supportive housing model has been credited with reducing chronic homelessness in the metro area over the past several years.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta homeless services and supportive housing

stories.headline: Georgia Legislature Wraps Messy 2026 Session: Tax Cuts Pass, Election Reform Stalls
stories.summary: Lawmakers passed a $38.5 billion budget and accelerated income tax cuts, but failed to resolve a looming crisis over ballot QR codes before a July 1 deadline.
stories.body:
Georgia lawmakers wrapped their 2026 legislative session after 1 a.m. Friday, passing a $38.5 billion state budget for fiscal year 2027 and approving accelerated income tax cuts while leaving some of the session's biggest issues unresolved.

The income tax bill will bring the rate from 5.19% to 4.99% faster than previously planned, with additional incremental cuts targeting 3.99% over time. It also gradually raises the standard deduction. A high-profile property tax relief proposal passed as well, though it was dramatically scaled back from its original form.

The biggest failure: the House and Senate could not agree on what to do about a self-imposed July 1 deadline to remove QR codes from ballot printouts. A 2024 election law mandated the change but provided no plan or funding for the transition. The House passed a compromise extending the deadline to 2028, but Lt. Gov. Burt Jones refused to put it to a final vote in the Senate.

That leaves Georgia potentially out of compliance with its own election law heading into the November midterms. Gov. Brian Kemp could call a special session before July 1 to resolve the impasse.

On other fronts, lawmakers passed a bill creating state oversight of homeowners' associations and expanded access to hormonal contraception. More than a dozen anti-LGBTQ+ bills failed, including HB54, which would have restricted gender-affirming care. A bill that advocates fear will increase incarceration of homeless people did pass.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia senators celebrate sine die at the end of the 2026 legislative session

stories.headline: Atlanta Celebrates 404 Day with Second Annual Parade Downtown
stories.summary: Hundreds turned out for the 404 Day parade from Peachtree Street to Underground Atlanta, with events across the city all day.
stories.body:
Saturday, April 4 is 404 Day, and Atlanta showed up for it.

The second annual 404 Day parade drew hundreds of participants and spectators as it wound a little more than a mile from Peachtree Street to Underground Atlanta. Events celebrating all things ATL continued across the city throughout the day.

The parade is part of a growing tradition embracing the area code as a point of civic pride. The date (4/04) has become an unofficial city holiday, with businesses, organizations, and residents using the occasion to celebrate Atlanta's culture, neighborhoods, and identity.

Weather cooperated with cloudy skies and temperatures around 80 degrees, though rain and cooler temperatures in the 60s are expected for Easter Sunday.
stories.imageAlt: Crowds at the second annual 404 Day parade in Downtown Atlanta

stories.headline: PUMA Brings World Cup Energy Downtown with Portugal Kit Launch
stories.summary: More than 200 fans gathered outside Mercedes-Benz Stadium for a community activation featuring live music from Lisbon, Portuguese food, and free jersey customization.
stories.body:
Before a single World Cup match kicks off this summer, PUMA brought the party to the people.

The global sportswear brand hosted a community activation on March 31 outside Mercedes-Benz Stadium at 285 Marietta St. NW, ahead of the United States vs. Portugal friendly. More than 200 fans turned out to celebrate Portuguese football culture and get a look at PUMA's 2026 Portugal National Team kit.

The event featured live music from Portugal-based DJ Firmeza, who was flown in for the occasion. Attendees got free pastel de nata from Nata Atlanta, sardines imported from Portugal, merchandise giveaways, and access to an on-site jersey personalization truck.

"We're trying to promote the kit itself for Portugal, but also create an environment for Portuguese fans to come out and just celebrate the World Cup starting here in the U.S.," said Colin Davis, senior producer at De-Yan, the creative production company that organized the event. "We really wanted to create a community-driven space."

The response exceeded expectations. A line formed early, and the energy stayed high throughout. It's a preview of the kind of international activations Atlanta can expect as the city prepares to host World Cup matches at Mercedes-Benz Stadium beginning in June.
stories.imageAlt: PUMA's Portugal kit launch activation outside Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta

stories.headline: Women's Comedy Film Festival Returns to 7 Stages Theatre
stories.summary: The seventh annual festival screened over 40 short films, hosted SAG panels, and served as a launchpad for emerging women filmmakers.
stories.body:
Caroline King keeps the Atlanta Women's Comedy Film Festival intimate by design. No hundred-dollar tickets, no endless lines, no impersonal premiere crowds.

"I really like to think of us as more of a grassroots boutique festival," King said. "It's intimate."

King founded the festival seven years ago as part of Cinema Life, her film festival collective that also produces the Southern Horror Film Festival, Georgia Shorts Film Festival, and Atlanta Women's Film Festival.

Filmmakers, comics, and enthusiasts gathered at 7 Stages Theatre in Little Five Points from March 27 through 29 for a full lineup: over 40 short films screened, table reads for chosen scripts, industry panels with the Screen Actors Guild, workshops, and award ceremonies.

The festival also serves as a launchpad for emerging talent. Maya Kimble, a recent Columbia College Chicago graduate, brought her thesis film "Shirker" for its premiere. She called it "very fitting" for the film to debut at the Atlanta festival.

"It's an all-encompassing event," King said. For a neighborhood built on independent arts and creative community, it fits right in.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta Women's Comedy Film Festival at 7 Stages Theatre in Little Five Points

stories.headline: DeKalb County Fixing 30-Inch Water Main Break Near Druid Hills
stories.summary: Watershed Management crews are investigating a major water main break at the intersection of North Druid Hills and Lawrenceville Highway.
stories.body:
DeKalb County Watershed Management is investigating a possible water main break at the intersection of North Druid Hills Road and Lawrenceville Highway, one of the busier intersections in the Greater Decatur area.

The break involves a 30-inch main, which is a significant piece of infrastructure. Crews are on site working to contain the break and assess the extent of damage.

A break of this size can affect water pressure and service for surrounding neighborhoods. Residents in the Greater Decatur and Druid Hills areas should be prepared for potential disruptions while repair work is underway.

DeKalb County's aging water infrastructure has been a recurring issue, with the county investing in upgrades across the system. No timeline for the repair has been announced.
stories.imageAlt: DeKalb County water main break repair at North Druid Hills and Lawrenceville Highway
